Re: Request for Formal- Advice
Dear Mr. Hallabrin:
In two Advice Letters to the former City Attorney for
t.lre City of Woodland (l,Ioe Advice Letters, A-90-580 and A-90-757)
you advised that two councilmembers, Mayor Elaine Rominger' and
Councilmember Joe Crescione, who owned property within 2500 feet
of a proposed development project, could participate in City
Council decisions related to the commencement of a needs
assessment in connection with the proposed project.
Your conclusion was based in large part on an
appraiser's determination that the decisions before the Council-
at that time would not have a foreseeable material financial
impact, distinguishable from the public generally, on the
councilmember's properties. you ended Advice lelter A-90-757
with the caution that the advice contained therein ,,was limited
in its application to the forthcoming decisions concerning the
t 
M.yor Rominger was identified in your previous advice
letters as councirmember Eraine Timothy. rhe oftice of mayor in
woodrand is not an erected position; c6unci_lmembers rotate
through the mayorship over time. wrrite serving u= *iyor, .
councilmembe'r has no additional authority ueyoia rrei iutrrority as
a councirmember, except to preside over and iun the meetings of
the City Council


proposed developnent, and specifically the council vote on
whether to proceed with the needs analysis. "
Subsequent to your ]-etters the council decided to
proceed with the needs assessment, which was completed January
21, 1992.t On May 7t 1992, the assessment was presented to the
Wood.Iand Pfanning Commission for its review. The Planning
Cotrmission voted five to zero, with one conmissioner absent and
one disqualifying herself, to accept the needs assessment and to
recommend to the City Council that it decfine to proceed further
with the Taormino Project at this time, No action has been taken
by any party with respect to the May 7 decision of the Planning
Commission. Unless the Planning Conmission's action is appealed
or otherwise forwarded, the City Council is not required to do
anything in connection with the needs assessment. It is
expected, however, that City Council will be asked to take some
action, 
Upon reviehr, the Council- could decide to do any of the
f oI-Lowing:
1) The Council may accept the needs assessment, lqL
accept the Planning Conmission recommendation, and direct staff
to process any applications received from the developer. The
Council is !9!, however, empowered to act upon the proposed
devel,opment at this time.
2\ The Council may simply vote to accept the needs
assessment without any directions to staff.
3) The Council may accept the recommendations of the
Planning Commission and direct. staff not to process any
applications received from the developer, effectively terminating
the project at this time.
This fetter constitutes a formal request for advice
from the FPPC \"rith respect to tlle duties of Mayor Rominger and
Councilmember Crescione in light of the particular facts set
forth below. This requesL is made pursuant to Government Code
Section 83114(b) and Title 2 of the California Code of
Regulations, Sections 18320 and 18329.
Scott Haflabrin, Acting General Counsel-
August 5, 1992
Page 2
9127.7-3
document, formally entitled
, are enclosed.
2 
Relevant
the "Planning Area excerpts from this
M Needs As ses sment ,,


Scott HaIIabri.n, Acting General Counsel
August 5, 1992

9t27.L-3
I. 
OUESTIONS PRESENTED
A. 
May Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Crescione, each of
whom own real property located within 2500 feeL of a proposed
development project, participate in decisions concerning the
project?
B. 
May Mayor Rominger, who has received more than $250
over the past twelve months from the YoIo County Farn Bureau (a
non-profit entity), but who is no longer employed by the entity,
participate in decisions concerning a proposed development
project when:
1. 
The Farm Bureau owns property adjacent to a parcel
of land identified as one of severaf possible alternative sites
for developnent of a similarly sized project?
2. 
The Farn Bureau strongly favors preserving
farmland within Yo.Io County, and the proposed deveLopment project
would involve the urbanization of prime agricultural- land?
C. 
May the Mayor participate in the review and update of
the City's General Plan, where the GeneraL Plan currently
includes policies intended to geographicalJ.y l-imit the City's
urban growLh and discourage the conversion of agricultural land
to urban uses ?
D. 
If the answer to the preceding question is no, may the
Mayor participate in the review and updating of those portions of
the City's General Plan not specifically related to the expansion
of the urbanized portion of the City or the conversion of
agricultural land to urban uses?
II. 
FACTS
A, 
Status of the Development Project.
The proposed development project, known as the
"Taormino Project" or "the Woodlands", cal1s for development of
275 si ngle fanj Iy homes and 28 acres of quasi-public facilities
on a 164 acre parcel located outside of both the present
boundaries of the City of the Wood]and ( "the City" ) and the


Scott Hallabrin, Acting General
August 5, 1992
Page 4
Counse I
9r27 . L-3
"urban limit fine" identified in the City's 1988 General Plan.3
As we indicated above, the Council will probably be asked to
review the Planning Commission's decision to accept the needs
assessment but recommend against proceeding with the project.
B. 
Ownership of Property,
Mayor Rorninger owns her own home at 703 Rubicon Place
Court, wiEhin 2,5000 feet of, but more than 300 feet from, the
proposed development. Councilmember Crescione owns his home at
L6!.4 Donner Way, within 2,500 feet of, but more than 300 feet
from, the proposed development. The enclosed apPraisal dated
July 1, L992, was prepared by an independent appraiser hired by
the City to evaluate the potential impact on the value of these
two properties of the decisions currently before the Council
concerning the Taormino Project.
C. 
Mayor Rominger's Former Employment.
we have also enclosed a copy of a letter (vrith
attachments) dated June 5, 1,992, from Mr. Alexander Froehlich, a
resident of the City. 
In addition to the question of whether
Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Crescione have a conflict of
interest because of the property each owns, Mr. Froehlich raises
concerns based on the relationship between Mayor Rominger and the
Yo.Lo County Farm Bureau ( "the Farm Bureau" ),
For purposes of this letter only, and except as
otherwise noted befow, we accept as substantially accurate the
factual statements contained within Mr. Froehlich's Ietter.
Although we endorse neither Mr. Froehlich's legal analysis nor
his conclusions, we have included the entire letter in the belief
that more information is better.
1. 
Mayor Rominger,s previous employment
by the Farm Bureau and her duties.
The Yolo County Farm Bureau is a California non-
profit mutual benefit corporation and a member of the California
Farm Bureau. Mayor Rominger was the Secretary-Treasurer and
Manager of the ['arm Bureau until she resigned those positions
3 
Defined as ,'tal
not planned or permitted.
necessarily coincide with
Area. " (Woodland Generaf
line beyond which urban development is
An urban limit Line does not
the boundaries of the ceneral plan
Pl-an--PoIicy P1an, p. 15. )


Scott Halfabrin, Acting General Counsel 
9127.L-3
August 5, 1992
Page 5
effective JuIy 15, :Igg2.4 The duties of the Secretary and
Treasurer are detailed in Article IX, 556 and 7 of the Farm
Bureau by-Iaws, a copy of which is attached to I"1r. Froeh.Lich's
Ietter.
As Mgnager, Mayor Rominger received income of more than
g250 per year,5 and was responsible for the day-to-day operation
of the Farm Bureau. Contrary to I'1r. Froehlich's suggestion, her
duties as l"lanager and Secretary-Treasurer did not involve the
formula!ion of policy or Farm Bureau positions on specific
issues,D although she did occasionally appear before the YoIo
County Board of Supervisors as an advgrcate of the policies and
positions adopted Ly the Farm Bureau.T The l-and uJe policies
advocated by the Farm Bureau include, as a general statement,
opposition to the urbanization of prime agricultural lands within
the County. The Farm Bureau rarely takes a position on issues
under consideration by the Woodland Planning Commission or City
Council, and has not taken a formal position on the Taormino
Project.
2, 
The farm Bureau's real property.
The Farm Bureau is a non-profit entity $rith gross
annual rece j-pts of approximately $200,000. It's principal asset
is a 8.34 acre parcel of real property located within the City of
Woodl-and. As required by the Generaf Plan, the needs assessment
identified and evaluated alternative sites where a large lot
4 
Mayor Rominger was first e]-ected to the Woodland City
Council" in 1990. She became a paid employee of the Farm Bureau
in 1986, four years before she was first elected to the City
Council . She became I"lanager of the Farm Bureau in 1987, three
years before her election to the City Counci}.
5 
A= ur. Froehlich notes in his letter, ltayor Rominger,s
husband is the President of the Farm Bureau and on its Board of
Directors. 
These positions are unpaid, however, and consequently
should not constitute a "source of income', to Mayor nomingdr.
6 
Fu.^ Bureau policies and positions are determined by
the Board of Directors and the voti-ng members of the Farm Bureau.
Mayor Rominger is noL now a member oi the Board of Directors.
, AJ-though the yolo.County Earm Bureau has not adopted
its.own county rand use poricies, it does advocate those land use
policies adopted by the California Farm Bureau,


Scott Ha.I labrin, Acting Generaf
August 5 t 1992
Page 6
Couns e 1
9127 . r-3
deve.Iopment on the scale proposed by the developer of the
Taormino Project could be located, I'he Farm Bureau property is
adjacent to one of several parcels discussed in the "alternative
Iocations" portion of the needs assessment (the "Aoki parcel").
(Needs assessment, pp. 50-51.) The Farm Bureau property itself
was not considered and is not the subject of any study or action.
In view of the fact that Mayor Rominger no longer
receives income fron the Farm Bureau , 2 CaL Code Regs.
Section LB702.5 appears inapplicable. In any event, there does
not appear to be a "substantial likelihood" that any of the
possible Counci.I decisions discussed above wilL affect the value
of the Earm Bureau property by $50,000 or nore,
D. 
Review of the Citv's General Plan.
The City's 1988 General Plan estab.Lishes certain
policies concerning development within the City's current
boundaries and expansion of those boundaries, These po.Iicies,
developed in conjunction with Yolo County, seek to maintain the
farn-oriented character of the City, to provide for orderly
growth, to ensure that adequate level-s of service wilL be
maintained, and to Iinit the conversion of prime agricultural
land to urban uses. These policies are implemented through the
use of phased growth areas and an Urban timit Line (defined in
footnote 3 above) .
The City Councif is currently considering whether to
begin the process of updating the General PIan, a project which
it is estimated wiII take up to two years. A fult update of the
ceneral Plan will necessarily include review of the policies
described above. The Council is not currently obligated to
commence a fuII update.
F. 
The Julv 1992 Independent Appraisal.
. 
The Ju.Iy 1, 1992 appraisal performed by Lynch &
Associates concluded that upon completion of the Ta6rmino
project, "In]o value increase wil-l be realized from the project
by either council members homes. Due to increased suppfj, oi
homes a slight decrease may be realized in the Z to :i'rln!e 
ot
under 95000," Accordingty. the conclusion reached in Advi6e
Letter A-90-757 stitl 
appears applicable, and neither l,tayoi
Rominger nor Councilmember Crescione should ne req"iieJ-i:o-
refrain from participating in Councif decisions p,ii""i"C-t"
2 Cal. Code Regs. Section-18702.3(a) (3) .


Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
August 5, 1992

9L27.t-3
we hope that the information provided in this letter
and t-he enclosures hereto will be sufficient, but if you have any
further questions concerning this matter, please do not hesitate
to call me.
S i ncere Iy ,

sections.question:
A. May Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Crescione, each of whom own real property located within 2500 feet of a proposed development project, participate in decisions concerning the project?

B. May Mayor Rominger, who has received more than $250 over the past twelve months from the Yolo County Farm Bureau (a non-profit entity), but who is no longer employed by the entity, participate in decisions concerning a proposed development project when:
1. The Farm Bureau owns property adjacent to a parcel of land identified as one of several possible alternative sites for development of a similarly sized project?
2. The Farm Bureau strongly favors preserving farmland within Yolo County, and the proposed development project would involve the urbanization of prime agricultural land?

C. May the Mayor participate in the review and update of the City's General Plan, where the General Plan currently includes policies intended to geographically limit the City's urban growth and discourage the conversion of agricultural land to urban uses?

D. If the answer to the preceding question is no, may the Mayor participate in the review and updating of those portions of the City's General Plan not specifically related to the expansion of the urbanized portion of the City or the conversion of agricultural land to urban uses?

sections.question_synthetic: Whether Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Crescione can participate in City Council decisions regarding the Taormino development project given their property ownership within 2500 feet of the project, and whether Mayor Rominger has disqualifying conflicts based on her former employment relationship with the Yolo County Farm Bureau and participation in General Plan review.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: This is an incoming formal advice request TO the FPPC (not a response FROM the FPPC). The document contains detailed Questions Presented and Facts sections but no Conclusion section, as it is seeking FPPC guidance. Document references prior FPPC advice letters A-90-580 and A-90-757. The sender is requesting advice pursuant to Government Code Section 83114(b).

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: A. May Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Crescione, each of whom own real property located within 2500 feet of a proposed development project, participate in decisions concerning the project?

B. May Mayor Rominger, who has received more than $250 over the past twelve months from the Yolo County Farm Bureau (a non-profit entity), but who is no longer employed by the entity, participate in decisions concerning a proposed development project when:
1. The Farm Bureau owns property adjacent to a parcel of land identified as one of several possible alternative sites for development of a similarly sized project?
2. The Farm Bureau strongly favors preserving farmland within Yolo County, and the proposed development project would involve the urbanization of prime agricultural land?

C. May the Mayor participate in the review and update of the City's General Plan, where the General Plan currently includes policies intended to geographically limit the City's urban growth and discourage the conversion of agricultural land to urban uses?

D. If the answer to the preceding question is no, may the Mayor participate in the review and updating of those portions of the City's General Plan not specifically related to the expansion of the urbanized portion of the City or the conversion of agricultural land to urban uses?

embedding.summary: This is an incoming request for formal FPPC advice from the Acting General Counsel regarding conflict of interest issues for Mayor Rominger and Councilmember Crescione in Woodland City Council decisions on a proposed 275-unit development project (Taormino Project), including concerns about property ownership within 2500 feet and Mayor Rominger's former employment with the Yolo County Farm Bureau.
